title: Leased Lines
description: Practice identifying leased line technologies including T1/E1, T3/E3, CSU/DSU devices, TDM multiplexing, and the hierarchical structure of digital signal levels used in dedicated WAN connections.
starterCode:
# LEASED LINES EXERCISE

## Part 1: Leased Line Basics

1. A _______ line is a dedicated, point-to-point WAN connection between two locations.
2. Leased lines provide _______ bandwidth that is not shared with other customers.
3. Leased lines use _______ Division Multiplexing (TDM) to combine multiple channels into one signal.
4. The bandwidth on a leased line is _______, meaning the same speed in both upload and download directions.
5. Leased lines are more _______ than shared connections but offer guaranteed performance.

## Part 2: T-Carrier System (North America)

6. A T1 line provides a total bandwidth of _______ Mbps.
7. A T1 consists of _______ DS0 channels, each carrying 64 Kbps.
8. DS0 stands for Digital _______ level 0, the basic unit of bandwidth in the T-carrier system.
9. A T3 line provides _______ Mbps of bandwidth, equivalent to 28 T1 lines.
10. The T-carrier system was developed by _______ (later AT&T) for digital telephone transmission.

## Part 3: E-Carrier System (International)

11. The E-carrier system is the international equivalent, used primarily in _______ and other regions.
12. An E1 line provides a bandwidth of _______ Mbps.
13. An E1 consists of _______ time slots (channels), each carrying 64 Kbps.
14. An E3 line provides approximately _______ Mbps of bandwidth.
15. E-carrier lines are standardized by the _______ (International Telecommunication Union).

## Part 4: CSU/DSU

16. CSU/DSU stands for Channel Service Unit/_______ Service Unit.
17. The CSU connects to the _______ provider's line and handles signal regeneration and diagnostics.
18. The DSU converts data from the router's _______ format into the format used on the leased line.
19. Modern routers often have _______ CSU/DSU functionality, eliminating the need for separate devices.
20. The CSU provides _______ testing capabilities, allowing the provider to test the line remotely.

## Part 5: Advanced Leased Line Concepts

21. A _______ T1 uses only some of the 24 channels, providing bandwidth in 64 Kbps increments.
22. _______ is the process of combining multiple T1 or E1 lines to increase bandwidth.
23. SONET stands for Synchronous _______ Network, a fiber-based transport standard.
24. An OC-3 SONET link provides approximately _______ Mbps of bandwidth.
25. Leased lines are being replaced by more modern technologies like MPLS, Metro _______, and SD-WAN.

## Part 6: Scenario - T1/T3 Line Provisioning

A bank is provisioning dedicated circuits between its headquarters and 5 branch offices to ensure secure, reliable connectivity for financial transactions.

26. A T1 line consists of 24 DS0 channels, each carrying _______ Kbps, for a total capacity of 1.544 Mbps.
27. A T3 line is composed of _______ T1 circuits, providing an aggregate bandwidth of 44.736 Mbps.
28. At the demarcation point, a _______ (Channel Service Unit/Data Service Unit) terminates the leased line and interfaces with the customer's router.
29. For smaller branches that only need 384 Kbps, the bank can order a _______ T1 using only 6 of the 24 available channels.
30. A _______ jack (also called a smart jack) is a network interface device at the demarc that allows the carrier to remotely test the circuit.

## Part 7: Scenario - Metro Ethernet vs Leased Line Migration

The bank's management wants to migrate from legacy T1 leased lines to Metro Ethernet for improved bandwidth and cost efficiency.

31. An Ethernet Virtual Private _______ (EVPL) provides a point-to-point Ethernet service that can carry multiple service instances over a single physical port.
32. MEF (Metro Ethernet Forum) defines three service types: E-Line for point-to-point, E-LAN for multipoint, and E-_______ for rooted multipoint.
33. Unlike fixed-bandwidth T1 lines, Metro Ethernet supports bandwidth on _______, allowing the bank to scale capacity without new physical circuits.
34. Traffic contracts specify the Committed Information Rate (CIR) as guaranteed bandwidth and the _______ Information Rate (EIR) as burst-capable bandwidth.
35. The last mile connection for Metro Ethernet can use either _______ optic cable for long distances or copper handoff for shorter runs.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Leased Line Basics

1. A **leased** line is a dedicated, point-to-point WAN connection between two locations.
2. Leased lines provide **dedicated** bandwidth that is not shared with other customers.
3. Leased lines use **Time** Division Multiplexing (TDM) to combine multiple channels into one signal.
4. The bandwidth on a leased line is **symmetrical**, meaning the same speed in both upload and download directions.
5. Leased lines are more **expensive** than shared connections but offer guaranteed performance.

## Part 2: T-Carrier System (North America)

6. A T1 line provides a total bandwidth of **1.544** Mbps.
7. A T1 consists of **24** DS0 channels, each carrying 64 Kbps.
8. DS0 stands for Digital **Signal** level 0, the basic unit of bandwidth in the T-carrier system.
9. A T3 line provides **44.736** Mbps of bandwidth, equivalent to 28 T1 lines.
10. The T-carrier system was developed by **Bell Labs** (later AT&T) for digital telephone transmission.

## Part 3: E-Carrier System (International)

11. The E-carrier system is the international equivalent, used primarily in **Europe** and other regions.
12. An E1 line provides a bandwidth of **2.048** Mbps.
13. An E1 consists of **32** time slots (channels), each carrying 64 Kbps.
14. An E3 line provides approximately **34.368** Mbps of bandwidth.
15. E-carrier lines are standardized by the **ITU** (International Telecommunication Union).

## Part 4: CSU/DSU

16. CSU/DSU stands for Channel Service Unit/**Data** Service Unit.
17. The CSU connects to the **service** provider's line and handles signal regeneration and diagnostics.
18. The DSU converts data from the router's **digital** format into the format used on the leased line.
19. Modern routers often have **integrated** CSU/DSU functionality, eliminating the need for separate devices.
20. The CSU provides **loopback** testing capabilities, allowing the provider to test the line remotely.

## Part 5: Advanced Leased Line Concepts

21. A **fractional** T1 uses only some of the 24 channels, providing bandwidth in 64 Kbps increments.
22. **Bonding** is the process of combining multiple T1 or E1 lines to increase bandwidth.
23. SONET stands for Synchronous **Optical** Network, a fiber-based transport standard.
24. An OC-3 SONET link provides approximately **155** Mbps of bandwidth.
25. Leased lines are being replaced by more modern technologies like MPLS, Metro **Ethernet**, and SD-WAN.

## Part 6: Scenario - T1/T3 Line Provisioning

26. A T1 line consists of 24 DS0 channels, each carrying **64** Kbps, for a total capacity of 1.544 Mbps.
27. A T3 line is composed of **28** T1 circuits, providing an aggregate bandwidth of 44.736 Mbps.
28. At the demarcation point, a **CSU/DSU** (Channel Service Unit/Data Service Unit) terminates the leased line and interfaces with the customer's router.
29. For smaller branches that only need 384 Kbps, the bank can order a **fractional** T1 using only 6 of the 24 available channels.
30. A **smart** jack (also called a smart jack) is a network interface device at the demarc that allows the carrier to remotely test the circuit.

## Part 7: Scenario - Metro Ethernet vs Leased Line Migration

31. An Ethernet Virtual Private **Line** (EVPL) provides a point-to-point Ethernet service that can carry multiple service instances over a single physical port.
32. MEF (Metro Ethernet Forum) defines three service types: E-Line for point-to-point, E-LAN for multipoint, and E-**Tree** for rooted multipoint.
33. Unlike fixed-bandwidth T1 lines, Metro Ethernet supports bandwidth on **demand**, allowing the bank to scale capacity without new physical circuits.
34. Traffic contracts specify the Committed Information Rate (CIR) as guaranteed bandwidth and the **Excess** Information Rate (EIR) as burst-capable bandwidth.
35. The last mile connection for Metro Ethernet can use either **fiber** optic cable for long distances or copper handoff for shorter runs.
hints: Leased lines are dedicated, point-to-point connections with guaranteed symmetrical bandwidth.
T1 = 1.544 Mbps (24 channels × 64 Kbps + 8 Kbps framing); T3 = 44.736 Mbps (28 × T1).
E1 = 2.048 Mbps (32 channels × 64 Kbps); E3 = 34.368 Mbps — used outside North America.
TDM (Time Division Multiplexing) divides time into slots, each channel gets a dedicated time slot.
CSU/DSU is the device that connects your router to the leased line — CSU faces the carrier, DSU faces you.
The CSU handles signal regeneration and loopback testing; the DSU handles data format conversion.
A fractional T1 lets you lease only the channels you need (e.g., 6 channels = 384 Kbps).
Bonding combines multiple T1/E1 lines into a single logical connection for more bandwidth.
SONET (Synchronous Optical Network) is a fiber standard; OC-3 = ~155 Mbps, OC-12 = ~622 Mbps.
DS0 (64 Kbps) is the fundamental building block — it was designed to carry one digitized voice call.

testCases.description: Should correctly explain mentions bonding concepts and implementation details
